articles.title: About this site
articles.description: Overview of my academic work and quantitative skills
articles.contents: Throughout my undergraduate studies, I have developed a strong interest in quantitative approaches to political science, which is reflected in the work collected on this website. Each project highlights different aspects of my ability to handle data, employ statistical techniques, and draw meaningful conclusions from empirical analysis. Rather than presenting isolated assignments, this collection traces my intellectual development, showing how my analytical skills and confidence with quantitative methods have evolved over time in the study of political science.

articles.title: Data Analysis Showcase
articles.description: A group of projects dedicated to the quantitative analysis of political data I did while studying module GOV300.
articles.contents:
Projects
Electoral Participation in the UK
An analysis of UK constituency-level election data to examine turnout, electorate size, and inequalities in political representation.This project examines whether voters are equally represented across UK constituencies.
Measuring Trust in the Central Bank
This project examines how trust in the central bank can be measured using survey data from the Q37 battery. It focuses on key issues in quantitative research, including missing data, reliability, and scale construction.
Who Isn’t Voting?
An analysis of a voter turnout across UK constituencies, with a particular focus on inequalities in political participation. By analysing patterns of turnout and non-voting, it highlights how variations in participation—especially across different social and geographic contexts—can shape electoral outcomes and raise important questions about representation in democratic systems.
← Back to Home

              at the University of Essex studying Politics and
              Economics. This website showcases my work from the GV300
              course, where I explore political phenomena through
              quantitative analysis. Across these projects, I work with
              real-world data, apply statistical methods, and present
              findings in a clear and accessible way. The portfolio
              reflects not only the outcomes of my work, but also my
              progression in developing analytical skills and confidence
              in using quantitative tools to understand political
              behaviour.

articles.title: Electoral Participation in the UK
articles.description: An analysis of constituency size, turnout, and unequal representation in UK elections.
articles.contents:
Overview
For this GV300 assignment, I analysed constituency-level election data from the United Kingdom to examine differences in electorate size and voter turnout. The purpose of the project was to evaluate whether constituencies are evenly sized and what that means for democratic representation. This matters because each constituency elects one Member of Parliament, even when the number of voters varies substantially between places.
Data preparation
The first stage of the project involved cleaning raw UK election data and transforming it into a constituency-level dataset suitable for analysis.
Show code


library(tidyverse)

uk_csv <- "https://electionresults.parliament.uk/general-elections/6/candidacies.csv"
uk_raw <- readr::read_csv(uk_csv, show_col_types = FALSE)

names(uk_raw) <- names(uk_raw) %>% 
  tolower() %>% 
  str_replace_all("\\s+", "_")

uk1 <- uk_raw %>%
  filter(!`election_is_by-election`)

uk2 <- uk1 %>%
  select(
    constituency = constituency_name,
    country = country_name,
    valid_votes = candidate_vote_count,
    electorate = electorate
  )

uk3 <- uk2 %>%
  group_by(country, constituency) %>%
  summarise(
    valid_votes = sum(valid_votes, na.rm = TRUE),
    electorate = max(electorate, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

uk <- uk3 %>%
  mutate(turnout_registered = 100 * valid_votes / electorate) %>%
  filter(is.finite(turnout_registered), electorate > 0)


Visualisation


ggplot(uk, aes(electorate)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5000, color = "white", fill = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Constituency Electorates in the UK",
    subtitle = "Most constituencies cluster around the middle, but some are noticeably smaller or larger",
    x = "Electorate size",
    y = "Number of constituencies"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 11)
  )



Not even distribution
The histogram shows that most UK constituencies are concentrated around a relatively similar electorate size, but the distribution is not perfectly even. Some constituencies are much smaller, while others are clearly larger. This matters politically because every constituency still elects one Member of Parliament, meaning that representation is not perfectly equal across places. Voters in smaller constituencies may therefore have more representational weight than those in larger ones.
Results of histogram analysis
The spread of results also indicates that the size of the constituency is not random, but is determined by institutional and geographical factors. For example, in rural areas or less densely populated areas, the electorate is often smaller, while in cities it is usually larger. This leads to a structural imbalance in representation, which is embedded in the electoral system.
Overall, the results suggest that although the UK electoral system strives to maintain equal representation, there is still significant inequality in practice. These differences highlight the importance of constituency boundaries and their structure for shaping political representation.
Key conclusion
Despite the fact that the constituencies should be the same in size, there are still differences. These differences mean that representation in the UK is not completely equal, which raises important questions about the fairness and structure of electoral systems.
Source
The full code and files for this project are available on GitHub:
View repository
← Back to Projects

articles.title: Measuring Trust in the Central Bank
articles.description: A project on reliability, missing data, and scale construction using the Q37 battery.
articles.contents:
Overview
For this GV300 assignment, I worked with a set of Q37 survey questions to explore key measurement issues in political and social research. The goal was not just to describe the answers, but also to evaluate whether a set of questions could be used as a reliable scale. This included examining missing data, checking internal consistency, and comparing different ways to build a composite metric.
Why this matters
Measurement is one of the most important parts of quantitative research. If a variable is poorly measured, even well-designed statistical analysis can become misleading. In this assignment, I focused on whether the Q37 battery provides a dependable way of measuring trust in the central bank and whether it should be combined into a single scale.
Exploring the battery
The Q37 battery contains several cells designed to measure the same basic concept. Before combining them, it is important to understand how many elements there are, whether they use a common response format, and how much data is missing.
Show code


load("run_unbanked.RData")




# your code here
library(tidyverse)
library(psych)


q37_names <- grep("^Q37_", names(run_unbanked), value = TRUE)
length(q37_names)

table(run_unbanked$Q37_1, useNA = "ifany")
table(run_unbanked$Q37_2, useNA = "ifany")


What this code does
This code identifies all the survey elements in the Q37 set and verifies the distribution of responses across the first two elements. It helps to determine whether a data set matches the overall scale and whether there are significant gaps that may affect subsequent analysis.
Missing data and reliability
One of the most important findings in this project is that missingness is not a minor issue. Only around 46.3% of respondents are complete on all Q37 items, meaning that listwise deletion would remove more than half of the sample. Another variable in the dataset, Q76, has about 61.1% missingness, which further illustrates how serious the missing-data problem can be. These patterns suggest that missingness should be diagnosed carefully rather than ignored.
At the same time, the reliability analysis produced a Cronbach’s alpha of approximately 0.93. This is a very high value, indicating strong internal consistency across the 12 items. In practical terms, this suggests that the Q37 battery is measuring a common underlying concept rather than a set of unrelated attitudes.
Show code


q37_complete <- complete.cases(run_unbanked[, q37_names])
percent_complete <- mean(q37_complete) * 100

mean(is.na(run_unbanked$Q76)) * 100

q37_num <- run_unbanked[, q37_names]
alpha_result <- psych::alpha(q37_num)
alpha_result$total$raw_alpha


Interpreting the results
The results show an ambiguous picture. On the one hand, the Q37 battery is highly reliable because the batteries are closely connected to each other. This suggests that they reflect a common underlying concept rather than measuring unrelated relationships. On the other hand, missing data is not trivial. If list deletion had been used, more than half of the sample would have been lost, which could have distorted the results or reduced the representativeness of the analysis. Taken together, these results indicate that the scale itself is quite reliable, but the lack of information about it should be taken seriously.
Building the scale
Building the scale
The assignment compared two ways of summarising the battery: a sum score and a mean score. Both contain the same underlying information, but the mean score is easier to interpret because it preserves the original 0–10 response scale. It is also more flexible when some items are missing, since a respondent can still receive a mean score even if they skipped one or two questions. For these reasons, I would prefer to report the mean score in a paper
Show code


q37_num <- run_unbanked[, q37_names]

sum_score_ex <- rowSums(q37_num, na.rm = TRUE)
mean_score_ex <- rowMeans(q37_num, na.rm = TRUE)

scale_df <- data.frame(
  sum_score = sum_score_ex,
  mean_score = mean_score_ex
)


Visualisation

Understanding the figure
The histogram shows how the respondents are distributed on the average confidence scale. Since the average score remains on the initial response scale from 0 to 10, it is much easier to interpret than the final score. Lower values indicate a lower level of trust, while higher values indicate greater trust. The distribution also makes it easier to see if the responses are grouped around a center or if there are significant differences between respondents.
Group Comparison
In the final part of the task, the scale of trust between the groups receiving education was compared. This does not establish a causal relationship, but it is useful for identifying patterns and thinking about how measurement may vary depending on social groups. In the assignment, education was used as a grouping variable for this comparison.
Show code


group_var <- "edu"

tmp_df <- data.frame(
  group = run_unbanked[[group_var]],
  mean_score = mean_score_ex
)

ggplot(tmp_df, aes(x = as.factor(group), y = mean_score)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "grey90") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  labs(
    x = "Education level",
    y = "Mean Q37 score",
    title = "Trust scale by education"
  )


The key conclusion
This project shows why measurements deserve close attention before embarking on any significant modeling. The Q37 battery is highly reliable, which allows you to combine the parameters into a single confidence scale. However, the large amount of missing data means that the scale construction is not only a technical problem, but also a potential source of distortion. In general, I would give the average score rather than the total, as it is more interpretable and better suited to data with incomplete answers to questions.
A source
This project is based on my GV300 Assignment 5 assignment on measurements, reliability, validity, and scale construction. The uploaded task describes the Q37 batteries, defect-free checks, reliability analysis, and scale construction steps.The main conclusion
This project shows why measurements deserve close attention before embarking on any significant modeling. The Q37 battery is highly reliable, which allows you to combine elements into a single confidence scale. However, the large amount of missing data means that the scale construction is not only a technical problem, but also a potential source of distortion. In general, I would give the average score rather than the total, as it is more interpretable and better suited to data with incomplete answers to questions.
A source
This project is based on my GV300 Assignment 5 assignment on measurements, reliability, validity, and scale construction. The uploaded task describes the Q37 batteries, defect-free checks, reliability analysis, and scale construction steps.

articles.title: Who Isn’t Voting? Turnout Inequality in the UK
articles.description: An analysis of voter turnout, non-voters, and participation inequality across UK constituencies.
articles.contents:
Overview
This project examines voter turnout across UK constituencies, focusing on who participates in elections and who does not. While previous analysis considered whether all votes are equal, this project shifts attention to turnout inequality and political participation. This is important because election outcomes depend not only on how people vote, but also on who actually turns out to vote.
Data preparation
I loaded data that in order to create structured turn out results the data must be clean.
Show code

uk_csv <- "https://electionresults.parliament.uk/general-elections/6/candidacies.csv" uk_raw <- readr::read_csv(uk_csv, show_col_types = FALSE)
names(uk_raw) <- names(uk_raw) %>% tolower() %>% str_replace_all("\\s+", "_")

Turn out categories
The cleaned dataset contains 650 constituencies across the United Kingdom.
Each observation represents a single constituency and includes information on:
country
constituency name
total valid votes
electorate size
turnout rate (percentage)
Main goal
A key finding of the analysis is that a large number of individuals do not participate in elections. In many constituencies, especially in large urban areas such as Birmingham, Manchester, and Leeds, tens of thousands of eligible voters do not vote.
Understanding the constituency varieties
Election outcomes reflect only those who vote, not the entire electorate. In areas with low turnout, representatives may be elected with support from only a small proportion of the population
Visualisation
Show code


ggplot(uk, aes(electorate)) + geom_histogram(binwidth = 5000, color = "white") + labs(
    title = "Distribution of Constituency Electorates in the UK",
    x = "Electorate size",
    y = "Number of constituencies"
)+ theme_minimal()



Interpretation
Most constituencies fall into the “Medium” turnout category, particularly in England. However, there is variation across regions. Scotland and Wales show more low-turnout constituencies, while Northern Ireland is more clustered.
These categories help summarise turnout but also simplify a continuous variable. This means they can hide more subtle differences between constituencies.
High vs low turnout
The comparison between high-turnout and low-turnout constituencies shows that turnout differences are not primarily driven by electorate size. Instead, they reflect deeper social and demographic factors.
Low-turnout areas tend to be more urban, younger, and economically disadvantaged, while high-turnout areas are often more rural, older, and affluent.
Key conclusion
The analysis demonstrates that political inequality is not only about differences in constituency size, but also about differences in participation. Large numbers of non-voters, especially in urban areas, suggest that electoral outcomes may not fully represent the preferences of the entire population.
Understanding turnout is therefore essential for evaluating the fairness and representativeness of democratic systems.
← Back to Projects
